file_name: WOMENSGOLFUNLIMITEDINC_03_29_2000-EX-10.13-ENDORSEMENT AGREEMENT - Intellectual Property Rights                 Confidentiality and Non-Use Obligations Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: (f) Recipient further acknowledges and agrees that all new discoveries, inventions, improvements, processes, formulae, designs, drawings, training materials, original works of authorship, photos, video tapes, electronic images, documentation, trademarks and copyrights (the "Intellectual Property"), that may be developed, conceived, or made by Recipient, alone or jointly with others during her work for the Company, shall be the exclusive property of the Company and shall be deemed a work for hire.
perturbation.changed_text: (f) Recipient further acknowledges and agrees that all new discoveries, inventions, improvements, processes, formulae, designs, drawings, training materials, original works of authorship, photos, video tapes, electronic images, documentation, trademarks and copyrights (the "Intellectual Property"), that may be developed, conceived, or made by Recipient, alone or jointly with others during her work for the Company, shall be the jointly-owned property of the Company and the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the agreement to state that intellectual property is 'jointly-owned' directly contradicts the 'work for hire' doctrine where the employer is considered the author and owner of the intellectual property. This creates ambiguity regarding ownership and control of intellectual property created during the endorsement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under 17 U.S. Code § 201, in the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author and owns the copyright. Stating it is jointly owned directly contradicts this.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Initial Ownership.— Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.— In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.— Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106, may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. (e) Involuntary Transfer.— When an i
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the 'work for hire' doctrine. The original text states that intellectual property created by the Recipient during their work is the 'exclusive property of the Company', aligning with the work-for-hire principle where the employer owns the copyright. However, the changed text makes the intellectual property 'jointly-owned' by the Company and the Recipient. This directly opposes 17 U.S. Code § 201 (b), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which states that 'In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright.' The change fundamentally alters the ownership structure established by the work-for-hire doctrine, creating a legal contradiction, therefore contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: (a) Recipient acknowledges and agrees that communications for the purpose of proposing to work for or working for the Company have in the past or will entail the disclosure, observation and display to Recipient of information and materials of the Company that are proprietary, confidential and trade secret, which include, but are not limited to, golf equipment marketing plans, research, development and designs, computer software, screens, user interfaces, systems designs and documentation, processes, methods, fees, charges, know-how and any result from the work performed by Recipient or the Company, new discoveries, Intellectual Property (as defined below) and improvements to the Company's products made for or on behalf of the Company (all of which, singly and collectively, "Information"). With regard to such Information, whether or not labeled or specified as confidential, proprietary or trade secret, Recipient agrees: (i) to use the Information solely for the purpose of making proposals to or working under contracts with the Company; and (ii) not to disclose or transfer the Information to others without the Company's written permission.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) Recipient acknowledges and agrees that communications for the purpose of proposing to work for or working for the Company have in the past or will entail the disclosure, observation and display to Recipient of information and materials of the Company that are proprietary, confidential and trade secret, which include, but are not limited to, golf equipment marketing plans, research, development and designs, computer software, screens, user interfaces, systems designs and documentation, processes, methods, fees, charges, know-how and any result from the work performed by Recipient or the Company, new discoveries, Intellectual Property (as defined below) and improvements to the Company's products made for or on behalf of the Company (all of which, singly and collectively, "Information"). With regard to such Information, whether or not labeled or specified as confidential, proprietary or trade secret, Recipient agrees: (i) to use the Information solely for the purpose of making proposals to or working under contracts with the Company; and (ii) to disclose or transfer the Information to others as Recipient sees fit.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the requirement for written permission to disclose confidential information, thus enabling the Recipient to share company trade secrets without consent. This contradicts the basic principles of trade secret protection, potentially violating laws related to misappropriation of trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Protection of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Under 18 U.S. Code § 1836, the unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets is a violation of federal law. By allowing the recipient to disclose information as they see fit, without the company's written permission, this agreement directly contradicts the protections provided under the Economic Espionage Act.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the protections provided by trade secret laws, specifically 18 U.S. Code § 1836, as it allows the recipient to disclose confidential company information without the company's written permission. This removes a critical control the company has over its trade secrets. The original contract requires written permission to disclose or transfer company information, while the changed text allows the recipient to disclose or transfer the information to others "as Recipient sees fit". This constitutes a clear violation by potentially enabling unauthorized disclosure, which goes against the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 which aims to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: (g) Recipient acknowledges and agrees that the Company possesses valuable know-how, proprietary, confidential and trade secret Information that has been procured or developed by the Company at great expense and that its unauthorized disclosure would result in substantial damages to the Company that may not be adequately compensated by monetary relief. Accordingly, Recipient hereby consents to the jurisdiction of the Federal and County Courts in Essex County, New Jersey and agrees that the Company may seek temporary restraining orders against it or other extraordinary relief necessary to protect the Information.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) Recipient acknowledges and agrees that the Company may possess know-how, information, and potentially trade secret information that has been procured or developed by the Company. The Recipient consents to the jurisdiction of the federal courts in New York County, New York.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the recognition of 'valuable know-how, proprietary, confidential and trade secret information' along with the specific harm of 'unauthorized disclosure'. Removing these elements challenges the basis for seeking specific relief like TROs. Additionally, changing the jurisdiction from New Jersey to New York could conflict with governing law clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause supports the ability to pursue claims based on the misappropriation of trade secrets under New Jersey law (N.J. Stat. Ann. § 56:4-1). By downplaying the existence and value of trade secrets, the changed text weakens the company's ability to enforce such protections under New Jersey law should the original agreement apply under choice of law principles.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation removes the explicit acknowledgement of 'valuable know-how, proprietary, confidential and trade secret Information' in the original contract. The law explanation suggests this weakens the company's ability to enforce trade secret protections under New Jersey law, specifically referencing N.J. Stat. Ann. § 56:4-1. While Snippet 1 discusses appropriation of name, brand, or trademark which is not directly related to the removed clause, the Law Explanation highlights the weakened ability to enforce trade secret protections, thereby creating a contradiction. The change deviates from legally recognized definitions concerning trade secrets, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable, which aligns with the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction definition. The alteration sufficiently undermines the legal obligation concerning trade secret protection, thus resulting in a contradiction.
